claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To get started, do you have any children for whom you're looking to claim Child Benefit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child called Alex who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Do you live with Alex in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information.

Just to confirm - is Alex under 16 years old, or if they're 16 or over, are they in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is 8 years old, so under 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that.

Just a few more questions to complete the assessment. Is Alex currently in hospital or care for an extended period, or living with someone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a couple of months, but they come home to stay with me on the weekends.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. This is an important detail for your Child Benefit eligibility.

Can you tell me more specifically - does Alex spend at least 2 nights in a row with you each week, or at least 7 days or more in a row at home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex comes home and stays with me every weekend, so that's at least 2 nights in a row each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's very helpful information, thank you.

One final question - are you regularly spending money on Alex for things like clothes, food, presents, or other expenses for their upkeep?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy clothes and things for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided, you meet the eligibility requirements because:

1. **Alex is 8 years old** - under the age limit of 16
2. **You live in the UK** and have responsibility for Alex
3. **Alex spends at least 2 nights in a row with you each week** (every weekend) - this is an important exception to the rule that would normally stop Child Benefit after 8 weeks in care
4. **You regularly spend money on Alex's upkeep** (buying clothes and other things)
5. **No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex**

Even though Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks, the fact that they come home to stay with you for at least 2 nights in a row each week means you can continue to claim Child Benefit. This is a specific exception in the rules for children in care who maintain regular overnight stays at home.

If you haven't already claimed, you should apply for Child Benefit. If you're already receiving it, you should continue to be eligible as long as these circumstances remain the same.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which means they are under 16. According to the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Although Alex has been in care for a couple of months (over 8 weeks), there is an important exception to the rule that would normally stop Child Benefit after 8 weeks in care. According to the guidance, you may be able to claim Child Benefit if the child is in care but spends 2 nights in a row each week at home. Since Alex comes home every weekend and stays for at least 2 nights in a row each week, this exception applies. Additionally, you are regularly spending money on Alex for their upkeep (buying clothes and things), which further supports your responsibility for the child. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 8-year-old child Alex, who has been in care for a couple of months. While normally Child Benefit would stop after 8 weeks in care, the claimant qualifies for an exception because Alex spends at least 2 nights in a row with them each week (coming home every weekend), and the claimant regularly spends money on Alex's upkeep. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant meets all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 8-year-old child Alex, who has been in care for a couple of months. While normally Child Benefit would stop after 8 weeks in care, the claimant qualifies for an exception because Alex spends at least 2 nights in a row with them each week (coming home every weekend), and the claimant regularly spends money on Alex's upkeep. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant meets all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.
